title: Strings
summary: Text you can measure, cut apart, and reshape.
refresher: A string is text in quotes: `"Ada"` or `'Ada'`. `len(s)` tells you how many characters it holds. You reach a single character with `s[i]`, and counting starts at 0, so the last character of a string of length 5 is `s[4]`. `s[-1]` is a shortcut for that last one. `s[1:4]` is a slice: it takes characters 1, 2, and 3, stopping before 4. A string cannot be changed in place, so build a new one instead.
refresher_code:
s = "Morgan"
print(len(s))
print(s[0])
print(s[-1])
print(s[0:3])
# 6
# M
# n
# Mor

blocks.body: A string is text. A name, a message, a line typed by a user. You write one by putting it in quotes, and Python then treats it as a single value you can store in a variable, print, or take apart.

blocks.caption: Single and double quotes do the same job. Having both is useful: if the text itself contains an apostrophe, wrap it in double quotes and you avoid a fight.
blocks.code:
school = "Morgan State"
motto = 'Growing the Future'
note = "It's a bear."

print(school)
print(note)

blocks.body: Two strings can be joined with a plus sign. This is called concatenation. It does exactly what it looks like: it sticks the second string onto the end of the first and hands you a new string.

blocks.caption: Notice the space inside the quotes. Python joins the characters you give it and nothing more, so if you want a gap between the words you have to put one there yourself.
blocks.code:
first = "Ada"
last = "Lovelace"
full = first + " " + last
print(full)

blocks.title: You cannot add a number to a string
blocks.body: `"Score: " + 10` raises a TypeError, because plus means one thing for text and something else for numbers, and Python refuses to guess which you meant. Two ways out. Convert the number with `str(10)`, or use an f-string, which is easier to read and is what most people reach for.

blocks.caption: An f-string is a string with an `f` in front. Anything inside the curly braces is worked out first, then dropped into the text. Values of any type are welcome, so no `str()` call is needed.
blocks.code:
name = "Ada"
score = 10
print(f"{name} scored {score} points")
print(f"Next year: {score + 5}")

blocks.body: Once you have a string, the first question is usually how long it is, and the second is how to get at one piece of it. `len(s)` answers the first. Square brackets answer the second, and this is where the counting gets interesting.

blocks.caption: Counting starts at 0, so the first character sits at index 0 and the last sits at `len(s) - 1`. A five character string has no index 5.

blocks.title: IndexError: string index out of range
blocks.body: `"Bears"` has five characters, so the valid indexes are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. Asking for `word[5]` asks for a sixth character that was never there, and Python stops the program rather than invent one. When you see this error, count the characters, then subtract one. That is the highest index you are allowed.

blocks.why: `len(word)` is 5, so this asks for `word[5]`. The valid indexes are 0 through 4, so there is no character at 5 and Python raises an IndexError. Picking "s" is the tempting answer, because `s` really is the last character. It just lives at index 4, not 5. To get the last character safely, write `word[len(word) - 1]`, or use the shortcut on the next screen.

blocks.body: The shortcut is a negative index. `s[-1]` is the last character, `s[-2]` is the one before it, and so on. It saves you the length arithmetic every time, and it reads better.

blocks.caption: Negative indexes count backwards from the end. There is no `-0`, so the countdown starts at -1. The last line shows the same character reached from both directions.

To take more than one character, use a slice: two indexes with a colon between them. It stops just before the second number, the same rule `range()` follows. Leave a side blank to mean "go all the way", so `[:3]` starts at the beginning and `[3:]` runs to the end.

Strings carry methods, which are actions you call on the string with a dot. `.strip()` cuts whitespace off both ends, which is what you want on anything a person typed. Notice the last line: `raw` still has its spaces, because each method returned a new string and nobody stored it.
blocks.code:
raw = "  Ada Lovelace \n"
clean = raw.strip()
print(clean.upper())
print(clean.lower())
print(clean.split())
print(repr(raw))
blocks.output:
ADA LOVELACE
ada lovelace
['Ada', 'Lovelace']
'  Ada Lovelace \n'

blocks.title: Catch the result or lose it
blocks.body: `name.upper()` on its own does nothing you can see. It builds a new uppercase string and then throws it away, because you never said where to put it. Write `name = name.upper()` if you want to keep it. Every string method works this way, so it is one habit that covers all of them.
That habit has a reason behind it. A string in Python is immutable, which means once it exists, its characters cannot be changed. You can build a different string, but you cannot edit this one.

blocks.caption: Assigning into a string raises a TypeError. Nothing you do to a string edits it in place, so the fix is always the same: build the string you want and put it back in the name.
blocks.wrong:
s = "cat"
s[0] = "b"
# strings do not
# support item
# assignment

blocks.code:
s = "  hello world  "
parts = s.strip().split()
print(len(parts), parts[-1].upper())
blocks.why: Take it one step at a time. `.strip()` trims the spaces on both ends, leaving `"hello world"`. `.split()` then breaks that at the space and gives you the list `['hello', 'world']`, which has 2 items, so `len(parts)` is 2. `parts[-1]` is the last item, `'world'`, and `.upper()` turns it into `'WORLD'`. If you picked 16, you measured the length of the string instead of the list: `len()` counts characters in a string but items in a list.

blocks.title: Habits that save you debugging time
items: Index of the last character is `len(s) - 1`, or just use `s[-1]`
A slice stops before its second number, exactly like `range()` does
Strip anything a user typed before you compare it: `answer.strip().lower() == "yes"`
String methods return a new string, so store the result or it is gone
Use an f-string whenever you mix text with a number, and skip `str()` entirely
Seeing an IndexError means you asked for a character past the end. Count the characters, subtract one, and check your index against that.
